results.title: Debunking AI Agent Fears: "Will humans get frustrated talking to AI?”
results.content:
Then they pick up on something. Maybe it’s the cadence. Maybe the voice responds just a fraction too quickly. They think, “have I been talking to a bot this whole time?”

They feel like a transaction. They either hang up or ask to talk to a live agent immediately.

### Human agents frustrate customers too

During human-to-human sales and support calls, frustration happens too. Every day.

Real reps put customers on hold for 15 minutes. They give inconsistent answers. They lose patience. They mishear or miss emotional cues.

AI doesn't eliminate frustration entirely. But it does give you the tools to quickly identify frustrations and know exactly where and how to address them.

What you need to do, is make sure:

With Regal, you have control over all of these factors. [...] How you can do this:

%20(1).webp)

In Regal, you can define exactly how your AI speaks, down to the phrase, and easily adjust based on customer feedback and performance trends.

The goal is to design your AI to feel like your best rep—clear, competent, and kind.

### #3 Make it easy to exit

You don’t want your contacts to ever feel “stuck” with an AI agent. If they’re frustrated, that will only make things worse.

There should always be a clear, simple way for them to exit any call and get transferred to a live agent.

That means:

.svg)

Regal allows you to build these exit and fallback conditions directly into the conversation flow, without code, to avoid dead ends and diffuse frustration before it occurs.

### #4 Ground your AI with real knowledge [...] ### Sentiment Score

Sentiment analysis tracks the customer’s tone throughout each interaction with your brand. You likely already have benchmarks you’re tracking towards for this.

Monitor sentiment when you start testing and launching AI Agents. If sentiment remains the same or improves, then the AI is not frustrating customers.

A downward trend after you’ve implemented AI could mean the AI isn’t picking up on emotional cues, and isn’t handling frustrations well. Consider refining the language for empathy or softening the voice pacing and tone, and see how that impacts sentiment.

### CSAT

Customer satisfaction scores can also be a general indicator of frustration across your contact center comms. If your CSAT remains within your target, the AI is doing its job.

#### Discussion about this post

H. Floyd

Apr 28

The CFO wire example is the one I would be nervous about in a real rollout.

The agent is not broken in the obvious sense. It is doing something it was allowed to do. The problem is that the business needs a way to narrow that permission while the run is still alive.

That is where I think the kill-switch conversation gets more interesting. [...] # Product Journey’s Substack

# You Can't Just Monitor AI Agents — You Need to Stop Them

### The Control Gap: Why Kill Switches Are Becoming the New Governance Layer

The Product Journey

Apr 09, 2026

Last week, we talked about drift.

Your AI agents degrade slowly over time. Performance drops from 92% to 84%. Nobody notices until customers complain.

But here’s a scarier question:

What happens when your agent isn’t drifting?

What happens when it’s making the wrong decision right now?

Not tomorrow.  
Not after the next monitoring cycle.

Right. Now.

Can you stop it?

## The Fraud Agent That Couldn’t Be Stopped

It’s 2:00 PM on a Tuesday.

Your fraud detection agent flags a suspicious transaction: $8,500 wire transfer to a new recipient in an unusual location. [...] “Stop the agent” is necessary, but it is too blunt to be the whole control model.

I would want a permission ladder written down before launch.

If one signal fires, the agent can still read but not write.

If another fires, it can draft but not send.

If a connector looks stale, quarantine that connector without killing the whole workflow.

If money, customer-visible claims, or destructive writes are involved, stop the run and preserve state for review.

That feels like the practical question under the dashboard layer:

When something looks wrong, which permission disappears first?

Share

results.title: AI Customer Frustration: 5 Signals Most Tools Miss | Twig
results.content:
### 1. Sentiment Signals (Text / Voice)

 Negative sentiment words: "terrible," "useless," "never," "disappointed"
 All-caps usage in text: "WHY IS THIS BROKEN"
 Exclamation point frequency above baseline
 Swearing (obvious signal)
 Voice signals: raised volume, faster speech rate, long pauses after product mention

### 2. Behavioral Signals (What They Did)

 Repeated contacts — 3+ tickets on the same issue within a week
 Re-opened tickets — customer clicked "my issue isn't resolved"
 Multi-channel attempts — filed a ticket AND chatted AND emailed on the same topic
 Support contact within 7 days of onboarding (often signals onboarding failure, a churn risk)

### 3. Engagement Signals (Before They Contact You) [...] ### What signals show a customer is frustrated?

Repeated contacts on the same issue, re-opened tickets, multi-channel attempts, negative sentiment in text, raised volume or long pauses in voice, failed knowledge base searches, and abandoned critical workflows.

### Can AI prevent support escalations?

Yes — by detecting frustration early and triggering proactive interventions like a priority bump, senior-agent routing, a proactive apology with SLA boost, or a retention offer. Detection without action is just a dashboard, so every signal should be paired with a response.

### What's the best AI tool for sentiment detection? [...] > TL;DR: AI detects frustration through sentiment markers, behavioral signals, and engagement signals — then triggers interventions like priority bumps, senior-agent routing, or proactive apologies. Top tools: Twig, Zendesk Advanced AI, Gladly. Focus first on repeat-contact detection and response-time breach alerts — these are the highest-signal, lowest-effort wins.

## What "Customer Frustration" Looks Like to an AI

AI doesn't feel emotions — it reads patterns in text, voice, and behavior that correlate with frustration. Those patterns cluster into three categories:

### 1. Sentiment Signals (Text / Voice)
